title: One thing I love about...Fallen Land 2nd edition
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dmbvfKzFSLo

A four-minute appreciation post from Daniel (@dungeondive), shot while setting up a solo game of Fallen Land 2nd Edition: the *one thing* he wants to single out today is the **documentation**, which he calls the gold standard for boxed games.

Four pieces work together. The **quick start guide** is a single bullet-pointed sheet that gets a lapsed player back into the game in minutes after a months-long break. The **rulebook** is the existing best-in-class doc, lightly perfected from first edition. The **scenario book** is the new piece — competitive, co-op, solo, and campaign-linkable variants stacked into one place. And then there's the **first player sheet**, which is the real star: each turn is conceptually a Council of Towns meeting chaired by whoever holds the sheet, and the sheet itself walks the chair through every phase — effects, town business, financial, NPCs, exploits, end-turn — in order.

The framing carries the whole pitch: documentation isn't a chore, it's a signal of designer confidence. If the team made *this* much effort to walk you through the game, they're confident the game survives the walk.

----

Daniel argues that great rulebook documentation is itself a *marketing* asset — that quality of writing and onboarding signals quality of design. Has bad documentation ever soured you on a game whose mechanisms you might otherwise have loved?
